front: Work: the title knight accepts a silken girdle that supposedly grants invulnerability; breaks the rules of a game by keeping it; receives a nick on the neck
back: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (Pearl Poet)
work: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

front: Work: uses a 'bob and wheel' metrical device — a short half-line of two syllables followed by a rhyming four-line stanza
back: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (Pearl Poet)
work: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

front: Work: Lady Bertilak tests the title knight by pretending to seduce him; animal carcasses are traded for kisses
back: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (Pearl Poet)
work: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

front: Work: the confrontation was planned by Morgan le Fay; the title knight must receive a return blow in a year and a day
back: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (Pearl Poet)
work: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

front: Work: a father grieves his lost daughter and dreams of seeing her as a maiden across a river representing death; she shows him the New Jerusalem
Work: the first five stanzas end with 'without a spot'; the narrator falls asleep in a 'garden green' on a mound covered in herbs
Work: uses images of Belshazzar's Feast and the Flood to illustrate the title concept of purity
Work: mocks Jonah for not possessing the title virtue, leading to his being swallowed by a whale; identifies the title virtue with poverty
Author: anonymous 14th-century Middle English poet; all four poems found in the Cotton Nero A.x manuscript
Author: part of the Alliterative Revival alongside Langland's Piers Plowman
Author: credited with a 352-line alliterative poem about St. Erkenwald, bishop of London
